title: Near-Infrared-Triggered Photodynamic Antibacterial Therapy Using Rose Bengal-Coated Upconverting Nanoparticles
authors: Pratik Deshmukh, Nandini Ahuja, Bhumika Sharma, Khageswar Sahu, Srinibas Satapathy, Shovan Kumar Majumder
abstract: Antimicrobial photodynamic therapy (aPDT) is a promising modality for inactivation of antibiotic resistant bacteria, relying on the activation of a photosensitizer (PS) by light of a specific wavelength. This process results in the formation of reactive oxygen species, which ultimately induce cell death. However, aPDT in its conventional form, is limited by the shallow penetration of visible light, restricting its effectiveness for treatment of soft tissue and orthopaedic tissues. To overcome this limitation, near-infrared (NIR) absorbing PS can be used. However, poor stability in vivo after injection, ineffective microbial targeting due to hydrophilic nature and off-site tissue damage are the issues with use of NIR absorbing bare PSs. This issue can be mitigated by combining NIR light with a upconverting nanoparticles (UCNPs), which mediate in conversion of NIR into visible light for effective PS activation. In this study, LaF$_3$:Er$^{3+}$,Yb$^{3+}$ nanoparticles were synthesized using a hydrothermal method and coated with Rose Bengal (RB), a promising hydrophilic PS for aPDT, to evaluate the potential for NIR-triggered aPDT. Characterization of the synthesized UCNPs confirmed the crystalline structure, size distribution and successful RB functionalization. Photophysical studies demonstrated efficient energy transfer between UCNPs and RB, leading to singlet oxygen ($^1$O$_2$) generation in vitro. Antibacterial studies against Methicilin resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), a superbug of implicated soft tissue and orthopaedic infections, revealed significant photo-bactericidal efficacy upon NIR irradiation, indicating the potential of RB-coated UCNPs for aPDT applications.
summary: This paper describes the creation of upconverting nanoparticles coated with Rose Bengal to enable antibacterial therapy using deep-penetrating near-infrared light. By converting NIR light into visible light, the system triggers the production of reactive oxygen species to kill antibiotic-resistant bacteria like MRSA. This approach addresses the penetration depth limitations of traditional photodynamic therapies.
main_problem: Developing an effective near-infrared (NIR)-triggered photodynamic antibacterial therapy to overcome the shallow tissue penetration of visible light and the instability of bare NIR-absorbing photosensitizers.
main_result: Successful synthesis of Rose Bengal-coated LaF3:Er,Yb nanoparticles that efficiently convert NIR light to visible light, enabling significant photo-bactericidal efficacy against MRSA via singlet oxygen generation.
method: Hydrothermal synthesis of lanthanide-doped LaF3 nanoparticles followed by coating with Rose Bengal; characterization via XRD, TEM, DLS, and FTIR; and antibacterial testing using the zone of inhibition method.
model_or_system: The experimental platform consists of LaF3:Er3+,Yb3+ upconverting nanoparticles (UCNPs) functionalized with a Rose Bengal (RB) coating, acting as a transducer for NIR-to-visible light conversion.
key_observables: Upconversion emission (green and red), singlet oxygen (1O2) luminescence at 1270 nm, hydrodynamic diameter, zeta potential, and zone of inhibition diameter.
important_parameters: 980 nm excitation wavelength, 2 mol% Er3+ and 20 mol% Yb3+ doping, and laser fluence ranging from 120 to 600 J/cm2.
main_assumptions: The efficiency of the therapy relies on the assumption of efficient Forster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) between the UCNP and the dye.
figures_summary: The figures document the crystalline structure (XRD), particle size and morphology (DLS, TEM, EDX), surface chemistry (FTIR), optical properties (UV-Vis, PL), singlet oxygen detection, and visual/quantitative antibacterial efficacy (zone of inhibition).
paper_structure: The paper follows a logical progression from material synthesis and structural characterization to optical property analysis (energy transfer efficiency) and finally to biological application (antibacterial assays).
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
